---
title: "Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control"
description: "How interpolation, finite differences, and quadrature all fit one numerical pattern: replace a hard continuous object by a simpler approximation, then reason explicitly about truncation, roundoff, and adaptive error control."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: numerical-methods
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/single-variable-calculus/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/concepts/floating-point-conditioning-and-backward-error.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain interpolation, numerical differentiation, and quadrature as approximation problems."
  - "Recognize truncation or discretization error as distinct from roundoff error."
  - "Understand why smaller step size does not improve accuracy forever."
  - "Interpret adaptive error control as balancing competing error sources against cost."
applications:
  - scientific-computing
  - engineering-systems
  - optimization
keywords:
  - interpolation
  - numerical differentiation
  - quadrature
  - truncation error
  - error control
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/single-variable-calculus/concepts/taylor-expansion.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/multivariable-calculus/concepts/multiple-integrals.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "Stanford CME108 syllabus"
  - "MIT 2.086 numerical differentiation notes"
  - "MIT 10.34 numerical integration lecture"
current-sources:
  - "Stanford CME108 syllabus pdf"
  - "Stanford CS137 syllabus"
  - "MIT 2.086 differentiation pdf"
  - "MIT 10.34 numerical integration lecture"
  - "Cornell CS4210 course page"
  - "Stanford CME108 bulletin"
---

## Role

This is the sixth page of the `Numerical Methods` module.

Its job is to show that interpolation, numerical differentiation, and numerical integration all share the same numerical pattern:

`replace a hard continuous object by an easier approximation, then manage the resulting error`

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why interpolation, finite differences, and quadrature are all approximation schemes
- why truncation error is different from roundoff error
- why making the step size smaller does not always improve the answer
- why adaptive error control is really a strategy for spending computation where it matters most

## Why It Matters

A huge fraction of numerical work is not about solving a linear system exactly.

It is about replacing an intractable continuous object by a simpler discrete one:

- replace a function by an interpolating polynomial
- replace a derivative by a finite difference
- replace an integral by a weighted sum

This always creates approximation error.

So the key questions become:

- what approximation family should we use?
- how quickly does the error shrink as the mesh or step size changes?
- when does roundoff start to compete with truncation error?
- how do we choose computational effort intelligently?

This is the core error-control viewpoint behind practical scientific computing.

## Prerequisite Recall

- Taylor expansion gives local polynomial approximations and remainder intuition
- floating-point arithmetic introduces roundoff at scale `\varepsilon_{\mathrm{mach}}`
- earlier pages in this module separated conditioning from algorithmic stability

## Intuition

### Interpolation

Interpolation replaces a complicated function by an easier surrogate that matches the function at selected points.

The hope is that:

- the surrogate is cheap to evaluate or integrate
- the mismatch between surrogate and true function is controlled

### Numerical Differentiation

Differentiation is sensitive because it magnifies small perturbations.

Finite-difference formulas use nearby function values to approximate a derivative, but they mix two errors:

- truncation error from replacing the derivative by a formula of finite order
- roundoff error from subtracting nearly equal numbers

### Numerical Integration

Quadrature replaces an integral by a weighted sum of function values.

The approximation improves when the rule matches the local function behavior well, but it still depends on step size, smoothness, and error accumulation.

### Why Error Control Matters

There is almost always a tradeoff:

- coarse discretization gives large truncation error
- excessively tiny steps can amplify roundoff or waste work

Good numerical computation is about choosing a scale where the total error is acceptable for the cost.

## Formal Core

::: {#def-interpolation}
## Definition: Interpolation

Interpolation chooses an approximating function `p(x)` so that

$$
p(x_i)=f(x_i)
$$

at selected nodes `x_i`.
:::

The approximation problem is then to understand how well `p` represents `f` away from the nodes.

::: {#def-finite-diff}
## Definition: Finite-Difference Approximation

A basic forward-difference approximation to `f'(x)` is

$$
\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}.
$$
:::

By Taylor expansion, this has first-order truncation error in `h` when `f` is sufficiently smooth.

::: {#def-quadrature}
## Definition: Quadrature Rule

A quadrature rule approximates an integral by a weighted sum

$$
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \approx \sum_{j=1}^m w_j f(x_j).
$$
:::

The nodes `x_j` and weights `w_j` encode the approximation scheme.

::: {#thm-error-split}
## Theorem Idea: Total Numerical Error Often Splits Into Competing Pieces

In many approximation schemes, total error can be understood as a combination of:

- truncation or discretization error
- roundoff error
:::

The useful step size is often the one that balances those contributions rather than minimizing either one alone.

::: {#thm-adaptive}
## Theorem Idea: Adaptive Error Control Uses Local Difficulty

Adaptive methods refine the grid or step size more where the function or solution is hard to approximate and less where it is easy.
:::

This is why adaptive methods often outperform uniform refinement at the same cost.

## A Small Worked Example

Take

$$
f(x)=e^x
$$

and approximate `f'(0)` using the forward difference:

$$
\frac{f(h)-f(0)}{h}
=
\frac{e^h-1}{h}.
$$

Using Taylor expansion,

$$
e^h = 1+h+\frac{h^2}{2}+O(h^3),
$$

so

$$
\frac{e^h-1}{h}
=
1+\frac{h}{2}+O(h^2).
$$

The exact derivative is `f'(0)=1`, so the truncation error is about `h/2` for small `h`.

This suggests smaller `h` is better.

But on a machine, if `h` becomes extremely small, the subtraction

$$
e^h-1
$$

can lose relative accuracy through cancellation, and roundoff begins to matter.

So the best practical `h` is not “as small as possible.” It is the scale where truncation and roundoff are reasonably balanced.

## Computation Lens

When you face an approximation scheme, ask:

1. what continuous object am I replacing?
2. what is the leading truncation or discretization error term?
3. how does roundoff enter the formula?
4. should I refine uniformly, or would adaptive refinement spend work more intelligently?

Those questions usually matter more than memorizing a menu of formulas.

## Application Lens

### Scientific Computing

Interpolation, differentiation, and quadrature are the building blocks behind simulation, data fitting, and time-stepping pipelines.

### Optimization

Finite-difference derivatives, local polynomial models, and error-controlled line-search or trust-region subroutines all rely on approximation language.

### Engineering Systems

Practical computation in mechanics, controls, and signal-processing pipelines depends on deciding how much approximation error is acceptable and where to refine.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- why these topics are all approximation problems
- why truncation error and roundoff error are different
- why step size should not be shrunk blindly
- why adaptive error control is about allocating computational effort intelligently

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

After this page, the next natural extension is:

- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)

The strongest adjacent pages are:

- [Numerical Methods](../index.qmd)
- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)
- [Taylor Expansion](../../../foundation/single-variable-calculus/concepts/taylor-expansion.qmd)
- [Optimization](../../optimization/index.qmd)
- [Multiple Integrals](../../../foundation/multivariable-calculus/concepts/multiple-integrals.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [Stanford CME108 syllabus pdf](https://cme108.stanford.edu/syllabus.pdf) - official syllabus covering interpolation, quadrature, and adaptive numerical thinking in one scientific-computing track. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS137 syllabus](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs137/syllabus.html) - official current scientific-computing syllabus with interpolation, numerical differentiation, and composite quadrature. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 2.086 differentiation pdf](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/2-086-numerical-computation-for-mechanical-engineers-fall-2014/d25e9d10e9cd4bd5dcc66422dedc0ada_MIT2_086F14_Nutshell_Diff.pdf) - official MIT notes for finite-difference approximation and its error analysis. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 10.34 numerical integration lecture](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/10-34-numerical-methods-applied-to-chemical-engineering-fall-2015/1d690d228d4051aa73175b567ef61c9a_MIT10_34F15_Lec16.pdf) - official MIT lecture notes for quadrature from interpolation and error viewpoints. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS4210 course page](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs4210/2014fa/) - official Cornell course page for the complementary numerical-analysis half focused on error analysis, approximation, interpolation, and integration. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1056641) - official course description linking these approximation topics to the broader scientific-computing curriculum. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [Stanford CME108 syllabus pdf](https://cme108.stanford.edu/syllabus.pdf) - `First pass` - official syllabus covering interpolation, differentiation, quadrature, and approximation-aware computation. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS137 syllabus](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs137/syllabus.html) - `First pass` - official current scientific-computing syllabus with interpolation, numerical differentiation, and composite quadrature. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 2.086 differentiation pdf](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/2-086-numerical-computation-for-mechanical-engineers-fall-2014/d25e9d10e9cd4bd5dcc66422dedc0ada_MIT2_086F14_Nutshell_Diff.pdf) - `First pass` - official MIT notes for finite-difference approximation and error analysis. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 10.34 numerical integration lecture](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/10-34-numerical-methods-applied-to-chemical-engineering-fall-2015/1d690d228d4051aa73175b567ef61c9a_MIT10_34F15_Lec16.pdf) - `First pass` - official MIT lecture notes for interpolation-based quadrature and integration error. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS4210 course page](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs4210/2014fa/) - `Second pass` - official Cornell course page for the approximation/interpolation/integration half of the numerical-analysis sequence. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1056641) - `Second pass` - official course description placing these tools inside a broader scientific-computing sequence. Checked `2026-04-25`.
